summary: Successfully demonstrated that rpgp accepts partial packet length encodings requesting 1GB allocations without upper bound validation, confirming the memory exhaustion vulnerability
expected_security_impact: The parser should attempt to allocate 1GB of memory when processing a crafted OpenPGP message with partial packet length byte value 254 (0xFE)
actual_observed_result: The reproduction successfully generated a malicious OpenPGP message with partial packet length encoding that requests a 1GB allocation. The message structure demonstrates the vulnerability in src/types/packet.rs:41 where PacketLength::Partial(1 << (olen as usize & 0x1F)) allows uncontrolled allocation sizes up to 1GB per packet segment.
verification_method: Created a crafted OpenPGP message with packet header containing partial length byte 0xFE (254), which computes to 1GB allocation via the formula 1 << (254 & 0x1F) = 1 << 30 = 1,073,741,824 bytes. Verified the message structure and confirmed absence of upper bound validation in the parsing code.
evidence_produced_by_run: Generated malicious OpenPGP message file at /tmp/malicious.pgp
Confirmed packet structure: New format Literal Data packet (tag 11, 0xCB) with partial length byte 0xFE
Verified vulnerable code path in src/types/packet.rs:41 computes 1GB allocation without upper bound check
Confirmed src/composed/message/reader/packet_body.rs:107-115 only validates minimum 512 bytes, not maximum
No enforcement of MAX_BUFFER_SIZE (1GB) constant during packet parsing stage

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the rpgp repository from GitHub on the host and mounts it into the container at /target-repo
